1.title: Bobby Locke wins second of four Open titles era
1.text: South Africa's Bobby Locke was the dominant figure in early-1950s major golf, capturing four Open Championships between 1949 and 1957 with his famed hooded putting stroke.

1.title: Mickey Wright wins first U.S. Women's Open
1.text: Mickey Wright captured the first of her four U.S. Women's Open titles in 1958, launching a career that produced 82 LPGA wins and 13 major championships.

1.title: Davis Love III wins MasterCard Colonial in fine form
1.text: Davis Love III was among the world's elite in the late 1990s, a stretch that produced his 1997 PGA Championship and helped establish him as a Ryder Cup mainstay and future captain.

1.title: Tiger Woods wins Memorial before historic U.S. Open
1.text: Tiger Woods holed a long birdie putt on the 72nd hole to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village, his final start before the 2008 U.S. Open at Torrey Pines.

1.title: Hideki Matsuyama wins Memorial in playoff
1.text: Hideki Matsuyama beat Kevin Na in a playoff to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village for his first PGA Tour victory.

1.title: Roberto De Vicenzo, Open champion, dies at 94
1.text: Argentine great Roberto De Vicenzo, winner of the 1967 Open Championship and famous for his 1968 Masters scorecard error, died on this day at age 94.

2.title: Craig Stadler born, 1982 Masters champion
2.text: Craig Stadler, born on this day in San Diego, won the 1982 Masters and 13 PGA Tour titles, earning the nickname 'The Walrus' for his mustache and burly build.

2.title: Dick Mayer, 1957 U.S. Open champion, dies
2.text: Dick Mayer, who beat Cary Middlecoff in a playoff to win the 1957 U.S. Open at Inverness, died on this day.

2.title: Jim Furyk wins Memorial Tournament
2.text: Jim Furyk captured the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 2002, a season in which he established himself among the world's top players.

2.title: Matt Kuchar wins Memorial Tournament
2.text: Matt Kuchar shot a final-round 70 to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village by two strokes over Kevin Chappell.

2.title: Patrick Cantlay wins Memorial Tournament
2.text: Patrick Cantlay closed with a 64 to overtake Adam Scott and win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village for his second PGA Tour title.

3.title: Hale Irwin born, three-time U.S. Open champion
3.text: Hale Irwin, born on this day in Joplin, Missouri, won three U.S. Opens (1974, 1979, 1990) and at 45 became the oldest U.S. Open champion when he won at Medinah.

3.title: Greg Norman dominates as World No. 1 era
3.text: Greg Norman spent a record 331 weeks as World No. 1 around this era, the charismatic Australian who won two Open Championships and reshaped golf's global popularity.

3.title: Tiger Woods wins Memorial Tournament
3.text: Tiger Woods won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 2001, one of his record-tying five victories in Jack Nicklaus's event.

3.title: K.J. Choi wins Memorial Tournament
3.text: K.J. Choi shot a closing 65 to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village by one stroke, becoming the first Asian-born winner of the event.

3.title: Tiger Woods wins Memorial to tie Nicklaus mark
3.text: Tiger Woods holed a remarkable flop shot on the 16th and won the Memorial Tournament for the fifth time, tying Jack Nicklaus for most career PGA Tour wins at 73.

3.title: Bryson DeChambeau wins Memorial in playoff
3.text: Bryson DeChambeau beat Byeong Hun An and Kyle Stanley in a playoff to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village for his second PGA Tour title.

4.title: Sandra Haynie born, four-time major winner
4.text: World Golf Hall of Famer Sandra Haynie, winner of 42 LPGA Tour titles including four majors, was born on this day in Fort Worth, Texas.

4.title: Gay Brewer wins Canadian Open
4.text: Gay Brewer, the 1967 Masters champion, captured the Canadian Open in 1972, one of his multiple PGA Tour titles.

4.title: Tiger Woods wins Memorial Tournament
4.text: Tiger Woods won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 2000, part of one of the greatest seasons in golf history that included a 15-stroke U.S. Open romp.

4.title: Carl Pettersson wins Memorial Tournament
4.text: Sweden's Carl Pettersson won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 2006 for one of his PGA Tour titles.

4.title: Jason Dufner wins Memorial Tournament
4.text: Jason Dufner shot a final-round 68 to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village by three strokes over Rickie Fowler and Anirban Lahiri.

4.title: Viktor Hovland wins Memorial in playoff
4.text: Norway's Viktor Hovland beat Denny McCarthy on the first playoff hole to win the 2023 Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village.

5.title: Willie Macfarlane wins U.S. Open over Bobby Jones
5.text: Willie Macfarlane defeated Bobby Jones in a 36-hole playoff at Worcester to win the 1925 U.S. Open, one of the longest playoffs in championship history.

5.title: Tom Lehman wins Memorial Tournament
5.text: Tom Lehman shot a closing 67 to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village by five strokes for one of his early PGA Tour victories.

5.title: Bart Bryant wins Memorial Tournament
5.text: Bart Bryant won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 2005 by one stroke over host Jack Nicklaus's event field for his second PGA Tour title.

5.title: Steve Stricker wins Memorial Tournament
5.text: Steve Stricker birdied the final hole to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village by one stroke over Brandt Jobe and Matt Kuchar.

5.title: William McGirt wins Memorial in playoff
5.text: William McGirt beat Jon Curran on the second playoff hole to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village for his first PGA Tour title at age 36.

6.title: Macfarlane completes marathon U.S. Open playoff
6.text: Willie Macfarlane's 1925 U.S. Open win over Bobby Jones required 36 playoff holes across two days at Worcester, one of the longest title deciders in U.S. Open history.

6.title: Tony Manero wins U.S. Open with record score
6.text: Tony Manero shot a record 282 to win the 1936 U.S. Open at Baltusrol, holding off Harry Cooper for a surprise victory.

6.title: Paul Azinger wins Memorial Tournament
6.text: Paul Azinger won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 1993, a season in which he would also capture the PGA Championship.

6.title: Ernie Els wins Memorial Tournament
6.text: Ernie Els won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 2004, adding a prestigious title during his run as one of the world's best players.

6.title: Justin Rose wins Memorial Tournament
6.text: Justin Rose won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 2010, the breakthrough that launched a stretch leading to his 2013 U.S. Open title.

6.title: Patrick Cantlay wins Memorial in playoff
6.text: Patrick Cantlay outlasted Collin Morikawa in a playoff to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village, defending the title he had won in 2019.

7.title: Olin Dutra contends in record U.S. Open era
7.text: Olin Dutra overcame illness to win the 1934 U.S. Open at Merion, one of the leading American professionals of the 1930s alongside Sarazen and Guldahl.

7.title: Hal Sutton wins Memorial Tournament
7.text: Hal Sutton won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 1986, one of the titles that marked him as a star of the 1980s.

7.title: Curtis Strange wins Canadian Open
7.text: Curtis Strange captured the Canadian Open in 1987, part of a stretch that saw him become one of the world's leading players heading into his U.S. Open titles.

7.title: Fred Couples wins Memorial Tournament
7.text: Fred Couples won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 1998, one of his many PGA Tour titles during a celebrated career.

7.title: Tiger Woods wins Memorial Tournament
7.text: Tiger Woods birdied the 72nd hole to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village by one stroke for another of his record five titles in the event.

7.title: David Lingmerth wins Memorial in playoff
7.text: David Lingmerth beat Justin Rose on the third playoff hole to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village for his first PGA Tour title.

8.title: Tommy Bolt era of fiery U.S. Open champions
8.text: Tommy 'Thunder' Bolt, famed for his temper and his swing, won the 1958 U.S. Open at Southern Hills as the championship grew into America's premier test of golf.

8.title: David Edwards wins Memorial Tournament
8.text: David Edwards won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 1992 for one of his PGA Tour victories.

8.title: Vijay Singh wins Memorial Tournament
8.text: Vijay Singh won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 1997, an early signature win on his path to becoming World No. 1.

8.title: Kenny Perry wins Memorial Tournament
8.text: Kenny Perry won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 2003, a hometown-favorite victory for the Kentucky native in Jack Nicklaus's event.

8.title: Lorena Ochoa dominates as World No. 1 era
8.text: Mexico's Lorena Ochoa reigned as the dominant force in women's golf in this period, holding the World No. 1 ranking for 158 consecutive weeks.

8.title: Hideki Matsuyama wins first PGA Tour title at Memorial
8.text: Hideki Matsuyama beat Kevin Na in a playoff to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village, becoming the first Japanese-born winner of the event.

9.title: Olin Dutra wins U.S. Open at Merion
9.text: Olin Dutra overcame illness and an early deficit to win the 1934 U.S. Open at Merion, overtaking Gene Sarazen down the stretch.

9.title: Lawson Little wins U.S. Open in playoff
9.text: Lawson Little defeated Gene Sarazen in an 18-hole playoff at Canterbury to win the 1940 U.S. Open, adding to his unmatched record as a double Amateur champion.

9.title: Tom Watson wins Memorial Tournament
9.text: Tom Watson won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 1996, ending a long victory drought on the PGA Tour.

9.title: Jim Furyk wins Memorial Tournament
9.text: Jim Furyk closed strong to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 2002 for one of his many PGA Tour titles.

9.title: Matt Kuchar wins Memorial Tournament
9.text: Matt Kuchar won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 2013, holding off Kevin Chappell by two strokes.

9.title: Patrick Cantlay wins Memorial Tournament
9.text: Patrick Cantlay closed with a 64 to overtake Adam Scott and win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village for his second PGA Tour title.

10.title: Big Three era reshapes golf worldwide
10.text: By the early 1960s Arnold Palmer, Jack Nicklaus and Gary Player had formed the 'Big Three' whose rivalries and global travels grew golf's popularity dramatically.

10.title: Greg Norman wins Memorial Tournament
10.text: Greg Norman won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 1990 during his reign as the World No. 1 player.

10.title: Tiger Woods wins Memorial Tournament
10.text: Tiger Woods won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 2001 for another of his record-tying five titles in Jack Nicklaus's event.

10.title: K.J. Choi becomes first Asian-born Memorial winner
10.text: K.J. Choi shot a closing 65 to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village by one stroke, becoming the first Asian-born champion of the event.

10.title: Tiger Woods ties Nicklaus with Memorial win
10.text: Tiger Woods's fifth Memorial Tournament victory in 2012 tied Jack Nicklaus with 73 career PGA Tour wins, second on the all-time list.

10.title: Bryson DeChambeau wins Memorial in playoff
10.text: Bryson DeChambeau beat Byeong Hun An and Kyle Stanley in a playoff to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village for his second PGA Tour title.

11.title: Ralph Guldahl wins second straight U.S. Open
11.text: Ralph Guldahl successfully defended his U.S. Open title at Cherry Hills in 1938, becoming one of the few players to win back-to-back championships.

11.title: Abe Mitchell, Ryder Cup trophy figure, dies
11.text: England's Abe Mitchell, a leading professional of the 1920s and the golfer modeled for the figure atop the Ryder Cup trophy, died on this day.

11.title: Cary Middlecoff wins first U.S. Open at Medinah
11.text: Cary Middlecoff shot 286 to win the 1949 U.S. Open at Medinah by one stroke over Sam Snead and Clayton Heafner, his first major championship.

11.title: Ralph Guldahl, two-time U.S. Open champ, dies
11.text: Ralph Guldahl, winner of consecutive U.S. Opens in 1937 and 1938 and the 1939 Masters, died on this day at age 75.

11.title: Greg Norman wins Memorial Tournament
11.text: Greg Norman won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 1995, one of the prestigious U.S. titles he captured during his career.

11.title: Jason Dufner wins Memorial Tournament
11.text: Jason Dufner shot a final-round 68 to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village by three strokes over Rickie Fowler and Anirban Lahiri.

12.title: Ralph Guldahl wins first U.S. Open at Oakland Hills
12.text: Ralph Guldahl set a U.S. Open scoring record of 281 to win the 1937 championship at Oakland Hills, the first of his consecutive titles.

12.title: Byron Nelson wins U.S. Open in double playoff
12.text: Byron Nelson outlasted Craig Wood and Denny Shute, then beat Wood in a second 18-hole playoff at Philadelphia Country Club to win the 1939 U.S. Open.

12.title: Mark Calcavecchia born, 1989 Open champion
12.text: Mark Calcavecchia, born on this day, won the 1989 Open Championship at Royal Troon in a four-hole playoff and captured 13 PGA Tour titles.

12.title: David Frost wins Canadian Open era contemporaries
12.text: The late 1980s tour was rich with international talent as South Africans, Australians and Europeans increasingly contended for America's biggest titles.

12.title: Tom Lehman wins Memorial Tournament
12.text: Tom Lehman shot a closing 67 to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village by five strokes for one of his early PGA Tour victories.

12.title: Bart Bryant wins Memorial Tournament
12.text: Bart Bryant won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 2005 for his second PGA Tour title.

13.title: Ben Hogan wins U.S. Open at Oakmont
13.text: Ben Hogan shot 283 to win the 1953 U.S. Open at Oakmont by six strokes, part of the legendary 'Triple Crown' season in which he won the Masters, U.S. Open and Open Championship.

13.title: Jack Nicklaus dominates the Memorial era
13.text: Jack Nicklaus founded the Memorial Tournament at his Muirfield Village course, which he designed, and it became one of the most prestigious non-major events on the PGA Tour.

13.title: Tiger Woods wins Memorial Tournament
13.text: Tiger Woods won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 1999, one of his record-tying five titles in Jack Nicklaus's event.

13.title: Ernie Els wins Memorial Tournament
13.text: Ernie Els won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 2004, adding a prestigious title during his run as one of the world's best players.

13.title: Justin Rose wins Memorial Tournament
13.text: Justin Rose won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 2010, the breakthrough that launched a stretch leading to his 2013 U.S. Open title.

13.title: Patrick Cantlay defends Memorial title in playoff
13.text: Patrick Cantlay outlasted Collin Morikawa in a playoff to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village, defending the title he had won in 2019.

14.title: Julius Boros wins first U.S. Open at Northwood
14.text: Julius Boros shot 281 to win the 1952 U.S. Open at Northwood in Dallas, the first of his two U.S. Open titles.

14.title: Fred Funk born, 2005 Players champion
14.text: Fred Funk, born on this day, won eight PGA Tour titles including the 2005 Players Championship at age 48, and became a multiple winner on the senior circuit.

14.title: Tommy Bolt wins U.S. Open at Southern Hills
14.text: Tommy 'Thunder' Bolt shot 283 to win the 1958 U.S. Open by four strokes over 22-year-old Gary Player at Southern Hills, the lone major of his career.

14.title: Fred Couples wins Memorial Tournament
14.text: Fred Couples won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village in 1998, one of his many PGA Tour titles during a celebrated career.

14.title: Tiger Woods wins fourth Memorial Tournament
14.text: Tiger Woods birdied the 72nd hole to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village by one stroke for another of his record five titles in the event.

14.title: David Lingmerth wins Memorial in playoff
14.text: David Lingmerth beat Justin Rose on the third playoff hole to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village for his first PGA Tour title.

15.title: Justin Leonard born, 1997 Open champion
15.text: Justin Leonard, born on this day in Dallas, won the 1997 Open Championship at Royal Troon and holed the famous putt that clinched the 1999 Ryder Cup at Brookline.

15.title: Jack Nicklaus wins fourth U.S. Open at Baltusrol
15.text: Jack Nicklaus opened with a record-tying 63 and finished with a record 272 to win the 1980 U.S. Open at Baltusrol, prompting the famous 'Jack is Back' scoreboard message.

15.title: Raymond Floyd wins U.S. Open at Shinnecock
15.text: Raymond Floyd, at age 43, shot a final-round 66 to win the 1986 U.S. Open at Shinnecock Hills, becoming the oldest U.S. Open champion to that point.

15.title: Ernie Els wins second U.S. Open at Congressional
15.text: Ernie Els closed out the 1997 U.S. Open at Congressional, edging Colin Montgomerie by one stroke to capture his second U.S. Open title in four years.

15.title: Jim Furyk wins U.S. Open at Olympia Fields
15.text: Jim Furyk shot 272 to tie the U.S. Open scoring record and win the 2003 championship at Olympia Fields by three strokes for his only major title.

15.title: Martin Kaymer wins U.S. Open at Pinehurst
15.text: Martin Kaymer dominated the 2014 U.S. Open at Pinehurst No. 2, opening with two 65s and winning wire-to-wire by eight strokes for his second major title.

16.title: Old Tom Morris born, 'Grandfather of Golf'
16.text: Old Tom Morris, born on this day in St Andrews, won four Open Championships and became a pioneering greenkeeper and course designer revered as the 'Grandfather of Golf.'

16.title: Lloyd Mangrum wins U.S. Open in double playoff
16.text: War hero Lloyd Mangrum won the 1946 U.S. Open at Canterbury, prevailing in a 36-hole playoff over Byron Nelson and Vic Ghezzi for his only major.

16.title: Ben Hogan wins U.S. Open at brutal Oakland Hills
16.text: Ben Hogan tamed the redesigned 'Monster' at Oakland Hills with a final-round 67 to win the 1951 U.S. Open, calling it one of his finest rounds.

16.title: Lee Trevino wins first U.S. Open at Oak Hill
16.text: Lee Trevino became the first player to shoot all four rounds under par in a U.S. Open, finishing at 275 to win at Oak Hill by four over Jack Nicklaus for his first major.

16.title: Phil Mickelson born, six-time major champion
16.text: Phil Mickelson, born on this day in San Diego, won six majors and at 50 became the oldest major champion at the 2021 PGA, but famously finished U.S. Open runner-up a record six times.

16.title: Andy North wins second U.S. Open at Oakland Hills
16.text: Andy North capitalized on a late collapse by T.C. Chen to win the 1985 U.S. Open at Oakland Hills for his second U.S. Open title.

16.title: Steve Jones wins U.S. Open at Oakland Hills
16.text: Steve Jones, returning from a near-career-ending injury, shot 278 to win the 1996 U.S. Open at Oakland Hills by one stroke over Tom Lehman and Davis Love III.

17.title: Tommy Armour wins U.S. Open playoff at Oakmont
17.text: Tommy Armour beat Harry Cooper in an 18-hole playoff at Oakmont to win the 1927 U.S. Open, the first of the 'Silver Scot's' three major titles.

17.title: Jack Nicklaus wins first major at Oakmont U.S. Open
17.text: Jack Nicklaus, a 22-year-old rookie, defeated hometown favorite Arnold Palmer in an 18-hole playoff at Oakmont to win the 1962 U.S. Open, his first professional victory and first major.

17.title: Johnny Miller shoots 63 to win U.S. Open at Oakmont
17.text: Johnny Miller fired a final-round 63, the lowest round in U.S. Open history at the time, to charge from six back and win the 1973 U.S. Open at Oakmont.

17.title: Hale Irwin wins second U.S. Open at Inverness
17.text: Hale Irwin shot 284 to win the 1979 U.S. Open at Inverness by two strokes, the second of his three U.S. Open championships.

17.title: Hale Irwin wins U.S. Open in playoff at Medinah
17.text: Hale Irwin holed a 45-foot birdie putt on the 72nd hole, then beat Mike Donald in a Monday playoff at Medinah to become, at 45, the oldest U.S. Open champion.

17.title: Retief Goosen wins U.S. Open playoff at Southern Hills
17.text: Retief Goosen three-putted the 72nd hole but recovered to beat Mark Brooks in a Monday playoff at Southern Hills for his first U.S. Open title.

18.title: Jack Nicklaus wins U.S. Open at Pebble Beach
18.text: Jack Nicklaus struck a 1-iron off the flagstick on the 17th hole to seal the 1972 U.S. Open at Pebble Beach, the first U.S. Open held at the storied seaside course.

18.title: Andy North wins first U.S. Open at Cherry Hills
18.text: Andy North made a clutch bogey on the 72nd hole to win the 1978 U.S. Open at Cherry Hills by one stroke, his first major.

18.title: Fuzzy Zoeller wins U.S. Open playoff at Winged Foot
18.text: Fuzzy Zoeller, who famously waved a white towel at Greg Norman on Sunday, beat Norman by eight in a Monday playoff to win the 1984 U.S. Open at Winged Foot.

18.title: Curtis Strange wins second straight U.S. Open at Oak Hill
18.text: Curtis Strange shot 278 to win the 1989 U.S. Open at Oak Hill, becoming the first back-to-back champion since Ben Hogan in 1950-51.

18.title: Corey Pavin wins U.S. Open with 4-wood at Shinnecock
18.text: Corey Pavin struck a magnificent 4-wood to the 72nd green at Shinnecock Hills to win the 1995 U.S. Open by two strokes, the lone major of his career.

18.title: Tiger Woods wins U.S. Open by record 15 strokes
18.text: Tiger Woods closed at 12-under 272 to win the 2000 U.S. Open at Pebble Beach by an all-time major-record 15 strokes, the most dominant performance in golf history.

18.title: Geoff Ogilvy wins U.S. Open as Mickelson falters
18.text: Geoff Ogilvy won the 2006 U.S. Open at Winged Foot when Phil Mickelson double-bogeyed the 72nd hole, the Australian's only major championship.

18.title: Brooks Koepka wins first U.S. Open at Erin Hills
18.text: Brooks Koepka tied the U.S. Open scoring record at 16-under to win the 2017 championship at Erin Hills by four strokes for his first major title.

19.title: Jack Fleck stuns Ben Hogan in U.S. Open at Olympic
19.text: Little-known Jack Fleck birdied two of the last four holes to force a playoff with Ben Hogan at the Olympic Club, setting up his shocking upset for the 1955 U.S. Open.

19.title: Hubert Green wins U.S. Open despite death threat
19.text: Hubert Green played the final holes of the 1977 U.S. Open at Southern Hills under a phoned-in death threat, holding his nerve to win by one over Lou Graham.

19.title: Michael Campbell wins U.S. Open at Pinehurst
19.text: New Zealand's Michael Campbell held off a late charge from Tiger Woods to win the 2005 U.S. Open at Pinehurst No. 2 for his only major championship.

19.title: Rory McIlroy wins U.S. Open with record at Congressional
19.text: Rory McIlroy set a U.S. Open scoring record of 16-under 268 to win the 2011 championship at Congressional by eight strokes, rebounding from his Masters collapse months earlier.

19.title: Dustin Johnson wins U.S. Open at Oakmont
19.text: Dustin Johnson played through a rules controversy over a moving ball to win the 2016 U.S. Open at Oakmont by three strokes, his first major championship.

19.title: Hubert Green, two-time major champion, dies
19.text: Hubert Green, winner of the 1977 U.S. Open and 1985 PGA Championship, died on this day, exactly 41 years after his Southern Hills triumph.

20.title: Ken Venturi wins U.S. Open battling heat at Congressional
20.text: Ken Venturi, fighting heat exhaustion in 100-degree conditions, completed a 36-hole final day to win the 1964 U.S. Open at Congressional for a triumphant comeback.

20.title: Jerry Pate wins U.S. Open with iconic 5-iron
20.text: Jerry Pate struck a dramatic 5-iron over water to two feet on the 72nd hole at Atlanta Athletic Club to win the 1976 U.S. Open in his rookie year.

20.title: Tom Watson chips in to win U.S. Open at Pebble Beach
20.text: Tom Watson holed a famous chip from deep rough on the 17th hole at Pebble Beach to beat Jack Nicklaus and win the 1982 U.S. Open, the only U.S. Open of his career.

20.title: Curtis Strange wins U.S. Open playoff at Brookline
20.text: Curtis Strange beat Nick Faldo in a Monday 18-hole playoff at The Country Club in Brookline to win the 1988 U.S. Open, his first major title.

20.title: Lee Janzen wins first U.S. Open at Baltusrol
20.text: Lee Janzen shot 272 to tie the U.S. Open scoring record and win the 1993 championship at Baltusrol by two over Payne Stewart.

20.title: Peter Thomson, five-time Open champion, dies
20.text: Australia's Peter Thomson, who won five Open Championships between 1954 and 1965, died on this day at age 88.

21.title: Gary Player wins U.S. Open to complete career Slam
21.text: Gary Player beat Kel Nagle in a playoff at Bellerive to win the 1965 U.S. Open, becoming the third player to complete the career Grand Slam.

21.title: Lee Trevino wins U.S. Open playoff over Nicklaus
21.text: Lee Trevino tossed a rubber snake at Jack Nicklaus on the first tee, then beat him in an 18-hole playoff at Merion to win the 1971 U.S. Open.

21.title: David Graham wins U.S. Open at Merion
21.text: Australian David Graham played a near-flawless final round 67, hitting every fairway, to win the 1981 U.S. Open at Merion, the first Australian to win the championship.

21.title: Scott Simpson wins U.S. Open at Olympic
21.text: Scott Simpson made three straight back-nine birdies to overtake Tom Watson and win the 1987 U.S. Open at the Olympic Club by one stroke.

21.title: Tom Kite wins U.S. Open in the wind at Pebble Beach
21.text: Tom Kite battled fierce wind and holed a chip on the seventh to win the 1992 U.S. Open at Pebble Beach, finally claiming his first major after years of close calls.

21.title: Scottie Scheffler born, future World No. 1
21.text: Scottie Scheffler, born on this day, rose to World No. 1 and won the Masters in 2022 and 2024 along with multiple Players Championships, becoming the game's dominant player.

22.title: Walter Hagen wins second U.S. Open in playoff
22.text: Walter Hagen beat Mike Brady in an 18-hole playoff at Brae Burn to win the 1919 U.S. Open, his second U.S. Open title.

22.title: Lou Graham contends in U.S. Open era of playoffs
22.text: The mid-1970s U.S. Opens featured frequent drama, including Lou Graham's 1975 playoff win over John Mahaffey at Medinah for his only major championship.

22.title: Dustin Johnson born, future World No. 1
22.text: Dustin Johnson, born on this day in Columbia, South Carolina, reached World No. 1, won the 2016 U.S. Open at Oakmont and the 2020 Masters, and amassed more than 20 PGA Tour titles.

22.title: Tiger Woods wins U.S. Open at Bethpage Black
22.text: Tiger Woods was the only player to finish under par at the demanding Bethpage Black, winning the 2002 U.S. Open by three for the second leg of a Grand Slam bid.

22.title: Lucas Glover wins rain-delayed U.S. Open at Bethpage
22.text: Lucas Glover won the weather-delayed 2009 U.S. Open at Bethpage Black, completed on a Monday, holding off Phil Mickelson and David Duval for his first major.

22.title: Martin Kaymer savors wire-to-wire U.S. Open at Pinehurst
22.text: Martin Kaymer's eight-stroke 2014 U.S. Open romp at Pinehurst No. 2 made him the first wire-to-wire champion since Tiger Woods in 2000.

23.title: Sam Parks Jr. born, 1935 U.S. Open champion
23.text: Sam Parks Jr., born on this day, shocked the field to win the 1935 U.S. Open at brutal Oakmont, the lone PGA Tour victory of his career.

23.title: Lawson Little born, double Amateur champion
23.text: Lawson Little, born on this day, won both the U.S. and British Amateurs in 1934 and 1935, then captured the 1940 U.S. Open as a professional.

23.title: Colin Montgomerie born, eight-time Order of Merit winner
23.text: Scotland's Colin Montgomerie, born on this day, won a record eight European Tour Orders of Merit and starred in the Ryder Cup, though he never won a major.

23.title: Julius Boros wins second U.S. Open at Brookline
23.text: Julius Boros won a three-man 18-hole playoff over Jacky Cupit and Arnold Palmer at The Country Club in Brookline to capture the 1963 U.S. Open at age 43.

23.title: Hale Irwin wins first U.S. Open at Winged Foot
23.text: Hale Irwin survived the brutal 'Massacre at Winged Foot' to win the 1974 U.S. Open at seven-over par, the first of his three U.S. Open titles.

23.title: Lou Graham wins U.S. Open in playoff at Medinah
23.text: Lou Graham defeated John Mahaffey in an 18-hole Monday playoff at Medinah to win the 1975 U.S. Open, the lone major of his career.

24.title: Johnny Farrell wins U.S. Open playoff over Bobby Jones
24.text: Johnny Farrell defeated Bobby Jones in a 36-hole playoff at Olympia Fields to win the 1928 U.S. Open, his only major title.

24.title: Billy Casper born, 51-time PGA Tour winner
24.text: Billy Casper, born on this day in San Diego, won 51 PGA Tour titles including the 1959 and 1966 U.S. Opens and the 1970 Masters, and is remembered as one of the finest putters in history.

24.title: Juli Inkster born, seven-time major champion
24.text: World Golf Hall of Famer Juli Inkster, winner of 31 LPGA Tour titles and seven majors, was born on this day in Santa Cruz, California.

24.title: Loren Roberts born, 'Boss of the Moss'
24.text: Loren Roberts, born on this day, won eight PGA Tour titles and several senior majors, earning the nickname 'Boss of the Moss' for his elite putting.

24.title: Retief Goosen completes U.S. Open playoff at Southern Hills
24.text: Retief Goosen recovered from a 72nd-hole three-putt to beat Mark Brooks in a Monday playoff at Southern Hills, claiming the 2001 U.S. Open for his first major.

24.title: Brooks Koepka wins second straight U.S. Open at Shinnecock
24.text: Brooks Koepka shot a final-round 68 to win the 2018 U.S. Open at Shinnecock Hills, becoming the first back-to-back champion since Curtis Strange in 1988-89.

25.title: George Sargent wins U.S. Open with record score
25.text: England's George Sargent shot a then-record 290 to win the 1909 U.S. Open at Englewood, New Jersey, by four strokes.

25.title: Gene Sarazen wins first U.S. Open at Skokie
25.text: Twenty-year-old Gene Sarazen closed with a 68 to win the 1922 U.S. Open at Skokie, his first major championship, edging Bobby Jones and John Black.

25.title: Gene Sarazen wins U.S. Open at Fresh Meadow
25.text: Gene Sarazen closed with rounds of 70-66 to charge from behind and win the 1932 U.S. Open at Fresh Meadow, completing a summer that also brought him the Open Championship.

25.title: Jack Nicklaus wins U.S. Open with record at Baltusrol
25.text: Jack Nicklaus shot a then-record 275 to win the 1967 U.S. Open at Baltusrol by four over Arnold Palmer, capping with a birdie on the 72nd hole.

25.title: Tiger Woods begins the 'Tiger Slam'
25.text: Tiger Woods's record 15-stroke 2000 U.S. Open win at Pebble Beach launched a run in which he captured four straight professional majors, holding all four titles at once by April 2001.

25.title: Wyndham Clark savors first U.S. Open win at LACC
25.text: Wyndham Clark won the 2023 U.S. Open at Los Angeles Country Club days before this date, holding off four-time major winner Rory McIlroy by one stroke for his first major championship.

26.title: Babe Zaharias born, founder of women's golf
26.text: Mildred 'Babe' Didrikson Zaharias, born on this day in Port Arthur, Texas, was an Olympic track champion who became golf's first female superstar, winning 10 majors and co-founding the LPGA.

26.title: John McDermott becomes first American-born U.S. Open champ
26.text: John McDermott, just 19, won a three-man playoff at Chicago Golf Club to become the first American-born winner of the U.S. Open and its youngest champion.

26.title: Jack Fleck completes shocking U.S. Open upset
26.text: Jack Fleck beat Ben Hogan in an 18-hole playoff at the Olympic Club to win the 1955 U.S. Open, denying Hogan a record fifth title in one of golf's greatest surprises.

26.title: Larry Nelson wins rain-hit U.S. Open at Oakmont
26.text: Larry Nelson shot a record-tying 132 over the final 36 holes to overtake Tom Watson and win the rain-interrupted 1983 U.S. Open at Oakmont, completed on a Monday.

26.title: Ernie Els wins first U.S. Open in playoff at Oakmont
26.text: Ernie Els won a three-way playoff over Loren Roberts and Colin Montgomerie at Oakmont to capture the 1994 U.S. Open, the first major of his career at age 24.

26.title: Michael Campbell holds off Tiger at Pinehurst
26.text: Michael Campbell's steady closing play held off a charging Tiger Woods to win the 2005 U.S. Open at Pinehurst No. 2, the New Zealander's lone major title.

26.title: Rory McIlroy savors record U.S. Open at Congressional
26.text: Rory McIlroy's record 16-under 268 and eight-stroke win at Congressional days earlier announced him as a generational talent at age 22.

26.title: Dustin Johnson wins first major at U.S. Open era
26.text: Dustin Johnson's 2016 U.S. Open win at Oakmont, sealed despite an in-round rules controversy, broke through after years of major heartbreak.

27.title: Bobby Jones wins Open and U.S. Open same year era
27.text: In 1926 Bobby Jones became the first to win both the U.S. Open and Open Championship in the same year, four years before his historic 1930 Grand Slam.

27.title: Ed Furgol's inspirational U.S. Open triumph
27.text: Ed Furgol, who played with a permanently bent left arm from a boyhood injury, had won the 1954 U.S. Open at Baltusrol days earlier for the lone major of his career.

27.title: Gary Player completes career Grand Slam at U.S. Open
27.text: Gary Player's 1965 U.S. Open win at Bellerive made the South African the third golfer, after Sarazen and Hogan, to win all four professional majors.

27.title: Payne Stewart era of emotional U.S. Open triumph
27.text: Payne Stewart's iconic 1999 Pinehurst victory, captured in the famous fist-pump image, came just months before he died in an aviation accident in October that year.

27.title: Graeme McDowell's breakthrough U.S. Open at Pebble Beach
27.text: Graeme McDowell had held off Gregory Havret to win the 2010 U.S. Open at Pebble Beach a week earlier, becoming the first European champion since Tony Jacklin in 1970.

27.title: Jon Rahm savors first major at Torrey Pines
27.text: Jon Rahm birdied the final two holes to win the 2021 U.S. Open at Torrey Pines days earlier, his first major title and a victory that returned him to World No. 1.

28.title: First U.S. Open era begins at Newport
28.text: The U.S. Open was first contested in 1895 at Newport, a 36-hole event won by Englishman Horace Rawlins that launched America's national golf championship.

28.title: David Graham caps precise U.S. Open win at Merion
28.text: David Graham's flawless 1981 U.S. Open performance at Merion, in which he hit every fairway in the final round, is celebrated as one of the finest ball-striking displays ever.

28.title: Tom Kite breaks through for first major at Pebble Beach
28.text: Tom Kite's 1992 U.S. Open win at a windswept Pebble Beach finally delivered the major that had long eluded one of the era's most consistent players.

28.title: Se Ri Pak inspirational LPGA era contemporaries born
28.text: The late 20th century produced a wave of international women's stars, led by South Korea's Se Ri Pak, whose 1998 U.S. Women's Open win sparked a golf boom in Asia.

28.title: Lucas Glover completes Monday U.S. Open at Bethpage
28.text: Lucas Glover's 2009 U.S. Open at Bethpage Black, finished on a Monday after rain delays, held off Phil Mickelson and David Duval for his first major.

28.title: Jordan Spieth's near-Grand Slam U.S. Open win
28.text: Jordan Spieth had won the 2015 U.S. Open at Chambers Bay days earlier when Dustin Johnson three-putted the 72nd hole, giving Spieth the second leg of a season that nearly produced the Grand Slam.

29.title: Willie Anderson wins second of four U.S. Opens
29.text: Willie Anderson won the 1903 U.S. Open at Baltusrol in a playoff, the first of three consecutive titles in his record four-win career.

29.title: Alex Smith wins U.S. Open breaking 300
29.text: Alex Smith won the 1906 U.S. Open at Onwentsia, becoming the first player to break 300 for 72 holes with a total of 295.

29.title: Jim Furyk wins U.S. Open week at Olympia Fields
29.text: Jim Furyk's record-tying 272 at Olympia Fields days earlier delivered the 2003 U.S. Open, the lone major of his consistent and decorated career.

29.title: Tiger Woods wins U.S. Open on a broken leg
29.text: Days earlier Tiger Woods won the 2008 U.S. Open at Torrey Pines while playing on a fractured leg, beating Rocco Mediate in a 19-hole playoff for his 14th major.

29.title: New generation reshapes the majors
29.text: By 2014 a new wave of talent led by Rory McIlroy, Jordan Spieth and Hideki Matsuyama was reshaping golf and the major championships.

29.title: J.J. Spaun's dramatic U.S. Open win at Oakmont
29.text: J.J. Spaun holed a dramatic 64-foot birdie putt on the 72nd green at Oakmont to win the 2025 U.S. Open days earlier, his first major championship.

30.title: Willie Park Sr. born, first Open champion
30.text: Willie Park Sr., born on this day in Musselburgh, won the inaugural Open Championship in 1860 and three more Open titles, founding a dynasty of champion golfers.

30.title: Chick Evans wins U.S. Open as amateur
30.text: Chick Evans set a U.S. Open scoring record of 286 while winning the 1916 championship at Minikahda, becoming the first to win the U.S. Open and U.S. Amateur in the same year.

30.title: Bobby Jones wins U.S. Open playoff at Winged Foot
30.text: Bobby Jones holed a long putt on the 72nd hole to force a playoff, then beat Al Espinosa by 23 strokes over 36 holes to win the 1929 U.S. Open at Winged Foot.

30.title: Payne Stewart wins U.S. Open playoff at Hazeltine
30.text: Payne Stewart beat Scott Simpson in an 18-hole Monday playoff at Hazeltine to win the 1991 U.S. Open, the first of his two U.S. Open titles.

30.title: Lee Janzen wins second U.S. Open at Olympic
30.text: Lee Janzen rallied from five strokes back on the final day to overtake Payne Stewart and win the 1998 U.S. Open at the Olympic Club, his second U.S. Open title.

30.title: Gary Woodland savors first major at Pebble Beach
30.text: Gary Woodland had held off defending champion Brooks Koepka to win the 2019 U.S. Open at Pebble Beach earlier in the month, holing a remarkable putt across the 17th green for his first major title.
